FACTS2
Company A is a consulting and project enltineer for the city
on Project x. In its role as consulting engineer and under a
contract with the city, conpany A will design and prePare the
plans and specifications for Project x. 
The city engineer will
ieview the work provided by the consulting engineer checking that
the design and specifications cornply with building codes and that
the design and specifications fu1fill 
the city's requirements
under the contract. After the city engineer's review, the design
and specifications will be used by the city to solicit bids fron
construction contractors for the construction of Project X.
In its role as project engineer, conpany A lrou1d on behalf of
the city interpret designs and plans, aqree to change orders and
price increases, decide upon construction materials and approve
the adequacy of the work done on Project X by the construction
contrac{or. 
company A would have the ultirnate responsibility for
overseeing the construction of Project x. 
The city will not
conduct an independent review of the decisions of the Project
engTineer.
Company B is a construction contractor and wants to bid on
the contiact for the construction of Project x. 
Two of the owners
of company A each own 33 l-/3 percent of colflpany B-

f. 
Public official

frorn making,
participating in naking, or otherwise.using his official 
Fosition
to influence a governmental decision in which the official 
has a
financial interest.

82048 to include every mernber, officer, 
employee or consultant of

provides under contract, information, advice,
recommendation or counsel to a state or loca1

iconsultant'r shall not include a person who:

for a specific project, we have advised that, for the purposes of
the Act the engineer is not a consultant because the exceptj-on of
187o0(a) (2) applies. (criss Advj-ce Letter, A-82-029, copY
encLosed. )
Essentially, the engineer j-s being -ca1]ed upon
to deliver a finished product -- a report, or
a design -- and not to participate in or
advise the District on general governnental
decisions requiring engineering expertise.
(Id. , footnote onitted. )
As consulting engineers, the individuals in Company A will
provide information, advice, recommendation or counsel to the city
under contract for a specific project- 
(Regrulation 18700(a).)
They will design and provide specifications and plans for Project
x. 
we assume that the principals of company A will not be subject
to day-to-day review or direction by the city, other than normal
contrict monitoring. Thus in their role as consulting engineers
the individuals in conPany A would not be Public off icial-s under
the Act and not be subject to the confl ict-of-interest 
provj.sions
of the Act.
B. 
The Individuals in companv A as Project Enqineers
whether the individuals in company A acting as project
engineers are public official-s wilt depend on the leve1 of their
aufhority. 
If the project engineers are to deliver sornething akin
to a finished product for a specific price, then they will not be
consuftants and therefore not public officials. 
(See criss Advice
Letter, supra.) If, however, the project engrineers possess
auttrority with respect to an agency decision beyond the rendition
of inforrnation, advice, recommendation or counsel'-- providing
nore than a finished product for a specific price -- the project
enqineers will be consultants. (Regulation 1870o(a) (2) (B).)
The latter appears to be the case here, More than providingl
information, advice, recornmendation or counsel, the project
engineers will be, j,n essence, making agency decisions. The
project enqineers have the power to bind the city. 
The project
engineers will review and accept the adequacy of the work
performed by the construction contractor. 
The project engineers
will also act on the city/s behalf on all matters relating to
design interpretation, change orders, price increases, and the
type of construction materials to be used.
Thus the individuals in Cornpany A will be consultants, for
purposes of the Act, and therefore public officiats, 
in two
scenarios. First, as consulting engrineers they will be
consultants if they are subject to the control or discretion of
the city when they perfor:o their work. (Regulation
18700(a) (2) (A).) 
Second, as project engineers they will be
consultants if they possess authority beyond the rendition of


Page
information, advice, recomnendation or counsel. (ReguLatj-on
18700 (a) (2) (B) . )
II. 
conflict of Interest
The following analysis assumes that the indivi-dua1s in
company A are public officials 
under the Act.-
The Act provides a four-part test to deternine whether a
public official 
has a conflict of interest in a particular
lovernrnental decision. First, is the official 
making, participat-
ing in uraking, or usinq his official 
position to influence a
qoiernnental decision? (section 87100.) second, is it foresee-
ilt" 
th.t the decision !tiI1 affect the official's 
economic inter-
est? (Section 87103.) Third, is the effect of the decision on
the official's 
economic interest naterial? 
(Id.) 
Fourth, is the
effect of the decision on the official's 
economic interest
distinguishable frorn its effect on the public aenerally? (Id.
87103.i (see Fishburn Advice Letter, No. A-90-386, coPy
enclosed. )
A. 
llakino A Governmental Decision
Regulation 18700 defines rrmaking a glovernmental decision'rl
Making a governmental decisiontr includes obligating or connitting
an age;cy to any course of action or entering into any contractual
agre6menl on behalf of an agency. (Regulation 18700(b) (3), (4)')
The project engineers will certainly be obligating or commit-
tj.ng the tity t" courses of action and entering into contractual-
agr6ernents oir b"ha1f of the city. 
on behalf of the ci'ty, the
pioject engineers can agree to.change orders, price increases, and
tU"'typ" oi construction materials used and can accept or reject
tte atlquacy of the construction contractor's wor[. These actions
rrill bi;d tle city and are governmental decisions.
The second issue is whether the decision will foreseeably
affect an econonic interest of the official. 
The Paraneters of
public official's 
economic interest are set forth in Section
87103. r'or the purposes of the matter at hand,
An official 
has a financial interest i-n a decision
within the meaning of Section 87100 if it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have
a material financial effect on the official 
or a
member of his or her immediate f arnily or on:
B.


our Fi,f e No.
Page 6
■-90-440
(a) Any business entity in which the public
official 
has a direct or indirect investment
worth one thousand dollars ($1,0oo) or more.
(c) Any source of incoroe, other than gifts
and other than loans 
aggregating two
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value
provided to, received by or pronised to the
public official within 12 months Prior to the
time when the decision is made.
(d) Any business entity in which the public
official 
is a director, officer, partner,
trustee, ernployee, or holds any position of
management.
(Section 87103. )
Three economic interests under the Act may be involved here'
rirst,--Lhe decisions may affect a.business entity, comPany B, in
*iri"tr'tir" princj.pals rnay have a direct investnent worth $1,000 or
nore. lseition -ezro:(a).). Second, the decisions nay affect a
source of it"o." to the principals of Conpany A. (Section
87103(c).) The principals are 33 -L/3 percent owners of conpany.B'

i"y-"if"tt 
a business entity, conpany B, in which the principals
,u| l" directors, officers, partners, employees or nay hold
po-sitions of management. (section 87103(d).)
The relevant inquiry then is whether decisions by the
principals in conpany A as project- engineers e,il1 have a
iL"i""iUfV foreseLable effect on their economic interests. 
The
"ti""i 
of'a decision is reasonably foreseeabLe if there is a
iuUstantial li,kelihood that it wil-l occur. To be foreseeable, the
effect of a decj-sion must be more than a mere possibility; 
. however
""rt"i-"ty is not required. 
(Downev cares v. Do$,nev comnUnitv
Developmlnt com. (1t87) 196 ca1 . App. 3d 983 , 989-991i wit! v'

ops. 198 copY enclosed. )
Given the facts you have presented, there is a substantial
Likelihood that decisions wi-l1 have a effect on the economi-c

of conplny B, the triggering econornic interest woul-d still

interests of the principals of conpany A. The principals of
company A will have the authority to accept or reiect the work of
compani s. 
The principafs of company A witl have the authority to
ansr+er design guestions by conpany B, agree to change orders and
price increases lrith conpany B, and determine the type of
-construction materials used by company B. There is a substantial
likelihood, if not certainty, that these deciBions will have a
financial effect on the principals' profits and conpany B's costs
and profits, 
An effect upon the principal-s' economic j-nterests is
reasonably foreseeable.
C. 
uaterialitv
The next issue is whether the financial effect is material.
To deternine if a decision's effect is rnaterial, we must first
determine whether the official's 
economic interest is directly or
indirectly involved in the decision. 
(Regulation 18702, copy
enclosed. )
we analyze each characterization of the officials' 
econonic
interests involved here. Each of the econonic interests may be
directly involved. A business entity !s directly i'nvolved- in a
governnEntal decision if the decision i-nvolves the approval or
deniat of any contract with the business entity. 
(Regulation
fa702.2(b).) The project engineers' decisions involving change
orders,'piice increases, constructj'on materials and acceptance of
project work appear to fall in this category.
An official's 
econornic interest is also directly involved if
a source of incorne is directly involved in the decision before the
official. 
(Requlation 78702.1(a) (1), copy enclosed.) Tha! is
clearly the-cale here. company B, a source of incone, would be
directly involved in decisions by the project enqineers on
contracl interpretation, change orders, price increases,
construction miterials, and the adequacy of company B's work'
Thus it appears that the pri.ncipals' economic interests are
directly invofvla in the governmental decision and the financial
effect is rnaterial. 
(Regutation 18702.1(a) (1).)
D. 
Publ-ic GeneraIlv
Even if the reasonably foreseeable financial effect of a
decision is material, disqual if ication is required only if the
effect is distinguishable from its effect on the public generally'
(Section 87103 . )
The decisions made by the engineers hlil] affect the
principals of company A particularly and differently fron the
&ecisi.6ns' effect upon the members of the public. 
As discussed
above, the principals' decisions will uniquely affect the rrork and
costs of Cornpany B and as a result how nuch the principals !ri11
receive in income as owners of conpany B. The decisions' effect
is distinguishable frorn the effect on the public generally.

fn summary then, under the facts you have presented, the
project engineers would have a conflict of interest.

interests of the principals of conpany A. The principals of

The next issue is whether the financial effect is material.
To deternine if a decision's effect is rnaterial, we must first
determine whether the official's

(Regulation 18702, copy
enclosed. )
we analyze each characterization of the officials'
